metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether _config_schema.py must be edited in THIS story to add `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` to VerifyConfig, or confirm that child 75de lands first on the feature branch before this story's tests run.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Enforcement means the boolean config key verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, owned by child 75de and defaulting false; tests toggle it through a temporary project config.'
VerifyConfig in _config_schema.py (lines 211-327) does not contain enforce_plan_material_pins
ACs 17-18: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true emits the complete pin membership' and '=false emits the complete pin membership' — both require the key to be parseable in this story's tests
_config_schema.py is not named as an edit target in this plan
findings.finding: The plan assigns verify.enforce_plan_material_pins to child 75de, but also says 'tests toggle it through a temporary project config' in THIS story. If the config key does not exist in VerifyConfig (_config_schema.py) until child 75de lands, this story's tests cannot write it to rebar.toml and read it back. The plan never names _config_schema.py as an edit target for this story, yet the AC and test claims require the key to be readable.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'Enforcement means the boolean config key verify.enforce_plan_material_pins'; Acceptance Criteria lines 17-18
scenarios: This story's tests write '[verify]\nenforce_plan_material_pins = true' to a temp rebar.toml but VerifyConfig silently ignores unknown keys (logs a warning), so the toggle has no effect and the tests vacuously pass without actually testing enforcement differences.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback persisted-format state for signing operations: specify whether old signing code (resign/refresh) is expected to be blocked from re-processing pin-bearing manifests, whether pin lines are preserved by old code, or explicitly acknowledge and document that pin-line loss on rollback-triggered re-signing is acceptable (and if so, add a DATA LOSS RISK note).
evidence: Plan describes pre-feature VERIFIER handling: 'a pre-feature-verifier fixture proving it authenticates the stored full DSSE manifest including unknown pin lines, then ignores only those lines for field interpretation'
Plan does NOT describe what old signing code (resign/refresh) does when it re-processes a pin-bearing manifest on rollback
T11 rubric: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
Absence: no section states 'after code rollback, pin lines in existing manifests are [preserved | acceptable to lose | guarded by X]'
findings.finding: The plan adds `plan-material-pin:` lines to the persisted manifest format and explicitly tests that a pre-feature VERIFIER can authenticate and ignore them, but it never asserts the post-rollback persisted format state for SIGNING operations. If old signing code (resign_plan_review, refresh_attestation) is re-deployed after new-format manifests exist, those operations will silently produce a manifest without pin lines, dropping pin data from the persisted artifact. The plan is silent on whether this constitutes acceptable data loss or what the canonical format state should be after such a rollback.
findings.location: Implementation §6 ('Extend build_manifest…Every plan-review signature-producing path…'); Verification §1 ('pre-feature reader authenticates…then ignores additive pin lines')
scenarios: Feature branch is deployed; several plan reviews are signed and stored with pin lines. A bug forces rollback to pre-feature code. An operator calls resign_plan_review on one of those manifests. Old build_manifest emits a manifest without pin lines, producing a new valid DSSE signature over a pinless manifest. Stored attestations silently lose pin provenance data.
Progressive drift refresh (refresh_attestation) is triggered automatically on pre-existing pin-bearing attestations by old code; all pin lines are stripped without any error or warning.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback section that states one of: (a) old signing code detects unknown manifest lines and refuses to re-sign (fast-fail, no data loss); (b) the feature branch is never partially deployed such that old code re-signs new manifests (deployment gate); or (c) pin-line loss on rollback re-signing is explicitly acknowledged as acceptable DATA LOSS RISK because pins are reconstructible from the graph on next forward-sign. Whichever path is chosen, add it to Acceptance Criteria with a matching test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the plan what repo_root=None resolves to (e.g., 'None falls back to the existing project config tracker path as returned by get_tracker_root()')  so an executor can implement it without a design decision.
evidence: 'collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id: str, repo_root=None) -> PlanRelationSnapshot'
'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker>'
No sentence in the plan describes what repo_root=None resolves to (e.g., from an existing config key, an env var, or CWD discovery).
findings.finding: collect_plan_relation_snapshot is specified with signature repo_root=None, and tracker_head_sha(tracker) is described using a caller-supplied tracker path, but the plan never states how the tracker directory is resolved when repo_root is None — leaving an executor without a specified default.
findings.location: Implementation bullet: 'calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id: str, repo_root=None)'
scenarios: An executor implementing collect_plan_relation_snapshot must invent a resolution strategy for repo_root=None and may choose differently from the intended behavior, causing tests to pass in dev but fail when signing from a different working directory.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'When repo_root is None, collect_plan_relation_snapshot resolves the tracker directory using the existing project configuration helper (e.g., get_tracker_root(repo_root)) — the same resolution already used by sign_plan_review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence to the Implementation body explicitly stating that both outgoing depends_on and inbound blocks (where another ticket's blocks relation names this ticket) are assigned role=prerequisite in related_material.
evidence: AC: 'Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite.'
Implementation: 'Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries across that full map' — no statement that inbound blocks map to role=prerequisite.
findings.finding: AC 8 ('Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite') asserts a specific mapping from inbound-block edges to role=prerequisite, but the Implementation body only says 'Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries' without ever stating that inbound blocks are assigned role=prerequisite. The normalization rule is asserted in the AC but unspecified in the body.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 8 / Implementation section 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot…'
scenarios: An implementer reading only the Implementation body assigns inbound-block edges to a different role or raises an error, causing AC 8 to fail without any spec text to correct them.
findings.suggested_fix: In the PlanRelationSnapshot paragraph, after 'Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries across that full map', add: 'Both edge directions are represented with role=prerequisite in related_material.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a role-assignment table co-located with the `PlanRelationSnapshot` spec in the Implementation section, explicitly stating: outgoing `depends_on` from subject → role `prerequisite`; inbound `blocks` targeting subject → role `prerequisite`; child (parent_id = subject) → role `child`.
evidence: AC 8 states 'Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite' — this is the decision rule.
Implementation §2 says 'Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries across that full map' but does not say which direction maps to which role.
The `related_material` tuple is 'the sole fingerprint carrier' and must carry the correct role — but the role-assignment protocol is only in the AC, not in the PlanRelationSnapshot schema definition.

MANAGED_REF_KINDS in _managed_refs.py confirms `blocks` and `depends_on` are distinct relation kinds (src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py:50), making the direction-to-role mapping load-bearing for correct behavior.
findings.finding: The normalization rule mapping both outgoing `depends_on` and inbound `blocks` to role=`prerequisite` is asserted only in AC 8 and is not co-located with the `PlanRelationSnapshot` dataclass definition or the `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` function specification in the Implementation section. An implementor writing `relation_snapshot.py` must infer from AC 8 that a subject's outgoing `depends_on` edge AND an inbound ticket's `blocks` edge on the subject both resolve to role `prerequisite` — but the Implementation body only says 'Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries across that full map' without stating the role assignment rule for each direction.
findings.location: Implementation §2 (PlanRelationSnapshot definition) vs. AC 8
scenarios: An implementor who reads only the PlanRelationSnapshot spec in Implementation §2 may assign role=`blocks` to inbound block edges rather than role=`prerequisite`, mismatching AC 8.

A subject ticket whose `depends_on` targets are treated differently from tickets that `blocks` the subject would emit inconsistent pin roles, violating AC 8 while appearing to satisfy the collect spec.
findings.suggested_fix: In the PlanRelationSnapshot definition paragraph, add: 'Role assignment: a ticket T whose parent_id equals the subject receives role `child`; a ticket T reachable via the subject's outgoing `depends_on` dep OR via an inbound `blocks` relation (T.deps contains `blocks` → subject) receives role `prerequisite`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify the exact behavioral distinction `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true` vs `false` produces at this story's scope (e.g., 'true causes store-pin-absence to be a hard error; false logs a warning'), or move ACs 17–18 and the config-key reference entirely to child 75de where the distinction is owned.
evidence: Plan text: 'Pin collection and snapshot-error handling are identical at true or false: new reviews always require complete pins, while only later validity treatment of drift changes.'
AC 17: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true emits the complete pin membership.'
AC 18: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false emits the complete pin membership.'

Verification: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py proves … emit complete pins with enforcement both enabled and disabled' — but the two ACs are semantically identical at this story's scope.
The 'later validity treatment of drift' is never specified — it is an undefined processing protocol referenced but not co-located with the config schema or enforcement logic.
findings.finding: The behavioral distinction between `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true` and `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false` is referenced in ACs 17-18 and the Implementation body but its decision protocol is never defined in this story — deferred to child 75de. ACs 17 and 18 assert that BOTH settings 'emit the complete pin membership', making them test identical behavior. The verification section says test_plan_review_resign.py 'proves … emit complete pins with enforcement both enabled and disabled' — but if the only observable difference at this story's scope is the enforcement key's existence and the pin set is identical either way, the test for the flag is vacuous (it cannot fail on the wrong value). The protocol that changes at enforcement=true — 'only later validity treatment of drift changes' — is never specified here, so an implementor cannot know what behavior to toggle, what tests of the *distinction* to write, or whether the config key being absent at false is acceptable.
findings.location: Implementation §5 ('Enforcement means the boolean config key verify.enforce_plan_material_pins…') and ACs 17–18
scenarios: An implementor writes `enforce_plan_material_pins` into the config schema but cannot specify its effect, producing dead config that tests cannot meaningfully differentiate.
ACs 17 and 18 will both pass on an implementation that ignores the flag entirely, making the AC pair unfalsifiable at this story's scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration-level test (or explicitly defer it to a named child story with a stated rationale) that exercises the full collect_plan_relation_snapshot → build_manifest → sign path against an actual (or fixture-backed) tracker repo, covering the happy-path pin-emission AC.
evidence: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot'
'Child 5967 separately runs the production-path revision-mismatch and atomic-signing integration tests; those behaviors are outside this story.'
Verification lists only pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py for ordinary sign/refresh/re-sign — no integration or E2E command against a real tracker.
findings.finding: The plan mandates that ALL signing paths (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, resign_plan_review) unconditionally call collect_plan_relation_snapshot — a hard cutover with an external git-tracker boundary — but the only proving commands for this path are unit tests. Child 5967's integration tests are explicitly scoped to revision-mismatch and atomic-signing only; no test exercises the basic happy-path collect+sign E2E against an actual tracker repo, and no explicit rationale is given for deferring that exercise.
findings.location: Verification section / Implementation bullet 'Every plan-review signature-producing path… calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot'
scenarios: An executor writes unit tests that mock tracker I/O, all pass, but the wiring of collect_plan_relation_snapshot into the real sign path is broken; the cutover bug ships silently.
E6(f) ANTI-FP requires explicit deferral to a named child with rationale; child 5967's stated scope does not cover the basic happy-path collection+sign flow.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a test in tests/integration/test_plan_material_pins_e2e.py that creates a minimal fixture tracker repo, calls sign_plan_review, and asserts the resulting manifest contains the expected pin lines; or add a sentence like 'Happy-path E2E signing integration is deferred to child XXXX which will run tests/integration/test_plan_review_signing.py' in the Verification section.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC stating: 'attest.py re-exports ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() at its public surface alongside existing manifest helpers.'
evidence: Implementation: 'attest.py imports and re-exports both beside the existing manifest helpers.'
Verification: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_material_pins.py tests/unit/test_attest_opcert.py proves … attest.py re-exports.'
No AC in the list reads 'attest.py re-exports ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins()'.
findings.finding: The Implementation describes a concrete deliverable — `attest.py imports and re-exports both [ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins()] beside the existing manifest helpers` — and the Verification test suite exercises it, but no Acceptance Criterion covers the attest.py re-export surface. The deliverable is orphaned from the AC list.
findings.location: Implementation section first paragraph / Verification section first bullet / Acceptance Criteria (absent)
scenarios: A reviewer signing off on AC completion would not check the attest.py public surface; a future refactor could silently drop the re-exports without failing any named criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the build_manifest backward-compatibility guarantee: calling build_manifest without the pins argument produces byte-identical manifest output to the pre-extension behavior.
evidence: Implementation: 'Extend build_manifest(…, *, pins: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] = ()) so absent pins preserve current manifest bytes exactly.'
No AC reads 'build_manifest called without pins produces byte-identical output to the pre-extension manifest bytes' or similar.
findings.finding: The Implementation states 'Extend build_manifest(..., *, pins: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] = ()) so absent pins preserve current manifest bytes exactly' — a specific behavioral guarantee about byte-identical output — but no Acceptance Criterion covers this property. The 'absent pins → unchanged bytes' guarantee is described work with no AC coverage.
findings.location: Implementation section sixth paragraph / Acceptance Criteria (absent)
scenarios: If a refactor breaks the byte-identity guarantee (e.g. whitespace normalization), no AC fails, but legacy manifest round-trips silently diverge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out / rollback note for the new PlanRelationSnapshotError signing failure mode — either a procedure to revert the signing-path wiring on the feature branch, or a documented operator action (e.g. repairing the stale relationship) that restores signing ability.
evidence: Implementation: 'if a referenced target was deleted after the prior PASS, refresh and re-sign return the same unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target remediation until the stale relationship is repaired, rather than preserving stale certification.'
Implementation: 'The feature branch is not merged or deployed between these stories.'
No section names a rollback procedure if this new failure mode causes widespread signing failures on the feature branch.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new failure mode into all signing paths (PlanRelationSnapshotError where signing previously succeeded) and explicitly acknowledges it ('if a referenced target was deleted after the prior PASS, refresh and re-sign return unsigned INDETERMINATE until the stale relationship is repaired'). However, no explicit rollback or back-out plan is described — the only implied rollback is 'do not merge the feature branch,' which is never stated. T4 requires the reversibility mechanism to be explicit, not implicit.
findings.location: Implementation section fifth/sixth paragraphs
scenarios: A ticket with a legitimately deleted prerequisite becomes permanently unsignable with no path to recovery short of repairing the relationship; operators on the feature branch have no documented back-out procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the O(store) preload rationale, the plan-material-pin manifest section contract, the PlanRelationSnapshot isolation constraint, and the new signing-pipeline stage so future stories have a canonical reference.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory' — novel O(store) pattern with explicit rationale that belongs in a design doc
'Define PlanRelationSnapshot ... in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py, which imports no signing or orchestration modules' — new typed boundary/pattern
'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear after optional verified-at-sha and before dep lines' — new manifest section format
'Every plan-review signature-producing path ... calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot ... immediately before building its new manifest' — new pipeline stage
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — an explicit O(store) full-map preload pattern for relation discovery, a new plan-material-pin manifest section with deterministic ordering semantics, a PlanRelationSnapshot typed boundary separating relation from signing concerns, and a new signing-pipeline stage — but specifies no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc capture for any of these decisions.
findings.location: Implementation section (all subsections); Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Future maintainers extending the relation model or manifest format lack a canonical explanation of the O(store) preload trade-off vs. targeted lookup, making it likely to be bypassed or duplicated incorrectly.
Onboarding engineers cannot distinguish intentional isolation constraints (no signing imports in relation_snapshot.py) from accidental omissions without documented rationale.
The manifest section ordering rule (after verified-at-sha, before dep lines) is a cross-component contract with no doc anchor, risking format drift across stories.
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/plan-material-pins.md (or an AGENTS.md section) covering: (1) why full-store preload is chosen over targeted lookup and its performance assumption, (2) the manifest section ordering contract as a format specification, (3) the import-isolation rule for relation_snapshot.py and its rationale, (4) the signing pipeline extension pattern. Reference this doc from the Implementation section of the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete motivation section (or a sentence in Goal) that shows what currently goes wrong without pins — e.g., 'today a refresh_attestation call can produce a valid signature over stale child material because there is no per-target fingerprint in the manifest; this story closes that gap.'
evidence: Goal: 'Bind every newly certified plan review to the narrow plan material of its direct children and direct prerequisites' — states desired behavior but not current failure mode.
No section describes what an attacker or drift scenario looks like today without pins, no 'currently a re-signed attestation can be verified against stale material' illustration.
No failing test, no Expected/Actual, no incident or defect reference is cited to motivate the addition.
findings.finding: The plan adds a significant new mechanism (plan-material pin collection, manifest encoding, error handling, tracker HEAD capture) but never demonstrates what currently goes wrong — it names no concrete defect scenario, provides no reproduction, and shows no Expected-vs-Actual gap in existing signing behavior that the pins would close.
findings.location: Goal section; Implementation section (overall)
scenarios: A plan reviewer reading this plan cannot tell whether the absence of pins has already caused a verifiable integrity failure, or whether this is a purely anticipatory control — which affects prioritization and scope decisions.
If pin collection is later shown to be unnecessary (e.g., the verifier already guards this), the wiring into every signing path would have been added without demonstrated need.
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend one paragraph to the Goal such as: 'Today, a plan-review manifest binds only the subject ticket's content; a reviewer re-signing after a child or prerequisite changes produces an identical manifest, making the prior certification indistinguishable from one conducted against current material. Pins close this gap by recording each target fingerprint at signing time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` to the plan's named edit targets and specify where `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` must be called within `resign_plan_review` (before the `attest.sign_plan_review` call at line 126).
evidence: Plan quote: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot immediately before building its new manifest'
resign.py:35 defines resign_plan_review(), which calls attest.sign_plan_review(verdict, material=current_material) at line 126 without any relation_snapshot call
resign.py is not listed anywhere in the plan's Implementation as an edit target
Verification section names test_plan_review_resign.py as proving resign emits complete pins — but the source file resign.py that needs editing is unmentioned
findings.finding: resign.py is never named as an edit target, but the plan mandates that resign_plan_review — which lives in resign.py and directly calls attest.sign_plan_review — must call collect_plan_relation_snapshot before building its manifest. The plan states 'Every plan-review signature-producing path — ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review — calls relation_snapshot.collect_plan_relation_snapshot immediately before building its new manifest', but resign.py is absent from the enumerated edit targets.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'Extend build_manifest'; also resign.py (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py)
scenarios: An implementer reading the plan would add collect_plan_relation_snapshot calls to attest.py (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation) but miss the resign.py modification needed for resign_plan_review, leaving the re-sign path pinless despite the AC requiring it.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add resign.py to the enumerated files: 'resign.py: call collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root) immediately after the staleness guard passes and before attest.sign_plan_review(verdict, …), propagating PlanRelationSnapshotError to the caller as unsigned INDETERMINATE.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (specifically `_run_plan_review`) and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` (specifically `drift_refresh`) as edit targets for adding the PlanRelationSnapshotError boundary catch that converts the error into a structured INDETERMINATE return.
evidence: Plan quote: 'The review boundary catches it before any LLM call, emits an ERROR record exactly {event: plan_relation_snapshot_error, reason: <enum>, canonical_id: string|null, reference: string|null}, and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE remediation through CLI and MCP'
__init__.py:489 defines _run_plan_review() which is the outer review boundary — it is not named as an edit target
orchestrator.py:645 calls attest.refresh_attestation(), making it another catch site — also not named
The plan only names attest.py and relation_snapshot.py as edit targets for this behavior
findings.finding: The plan introduces collect_plan_relation_snapshot as a new function in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py called from sign_plan_review and refresh_attestation (both in attest.py), and from resign_plan_review (resign.py). These callers live inside src/rebar/llm/plan_review/. However, the structured ERROR record ('emits an ERROR record exactly {event: plan_relation_snapshot_error, …}, and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE') must be produced by the 'review boundary' — which is the orchestrator or the __init__.py review_plan entrypoint. Neither orchestrator.py nor __init__.py is named as an edit target for adding the PlanRelationSnapshotError catch, yet those are the consumers that must catch and convert the error before any LLM call.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation'; also src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py and orchestrator.py
scenarios: The implementer adds PlanRelationSnapshotError raises inside relation_snapshot.py and sign_plan_review in attest.py, but without a catch in _run_plan_review() or orchestrator.drift_refresh(), the exception propagates as an unhandled exception rather than a structured INDETERMINATE return, violating 'the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the implementation text explaining why the single O(store) preload is chosen (e.g. it avoids N+1 per-target reductions, enables a single consistent snapshot for all targets, and is simpler than a targeted archived-inclusive scan of the full store).
evidence: The plan explains why find_inbound_relationships is excluded ('its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets') but does not state why the O(store) preload is preferable to, e.g., a targeted scan with archived-inclusion or a lighter per-target read.
A plausible alternative — extending find_inbound_relationships to accept an include_archived flag — is structurally different and is never addressed or acknowledged in the plan.
G6 criterion requires: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding)'
findings.finding: The plan states a negative rationale for not calling `find_inbound_relationships` (its active-source filter omits archived tickets) but provides no positive rationale for why the O(store) preload approach is the right fit — violating the G6 requirement that the plan states a positive rationale for the chosen approach.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states; this explicit O(store) map is required to discover arbitrary inbound blockers. ... do not call find_inbound_relationships because its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets. Resolve aliases and prefixes before deduplication; do not repeat store-wide reductions per target.'
scenarios: An implementer choosing between the O(store) preload and a targeted per-relation scan has no stated guidance from the plan on why the preload approach was selected.
Future maintainers may not understand why a cheaper targeted scan was not used, leading to premature optimization that reintroduces the archived-ticket gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence such as: 'The O(store) single-pass preload is preferred over N+1 per-target reductions because it provides a consistent snapshot of all ticket states (including archived) at one point in time, avoids redundant I/O when multiple targets share the same source, and is simpler than maintaining a targeted archived-inclusive variant of find_inbound_relationships.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prior-art rationale to the Implementation section explaining why `plan-material-pin:` text lines were chosen over in-toto link `materials` fields or a structured predicate extension, given ADR 0044/0049 explicitly adopted in-toto/DSSE as the attestation substrate for this project.
evidence: Plan quote: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — a custom plaintext manifest line
docs/adr/0044-asymmetric-attestation-substrate.md: 'DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard; the encoder is ~30 lines, offline, scheme-agnostic.'
docs/adr/0049-opcert-asymmetric.md prior-art section: 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance produced by a trusted control plane'
src/rebar/signing.py L228-231: verified-at-sha step is 'shaped as an in-toto-style subject so a future move to a DSSE/asymmetric envelope is an envelope swap, not a data-shape rewrite'
in-toto link attestations define a 'materials' map ({artifact-name: {sha256: hex}}) — the exact 'which inputs did this step consume' semantic the pin lines encode
ABSENCE: no mention in the plan of in-toto link materials, SLSA provenance byProducts/materials, or why a structured predicate field was not used
src/rebar/attest/ already ships a full DSSE envelope + in-toto-aligned Statement/predicate substrate (opcert.py, dsse.py)
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom `plan-material-pin: <role> <id> <fingerprint>` plaintext manifest-line format to record which related-ticket material a review relied on — precisely the 'materials used' provenance that in-toto link attestations already model. The project's own ADR 0044 adopted DSSE/in-toto as its signing substrate ('DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard') and ADR 0049 explicitly cites 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance' as prior art for op-certs. The `verified-at-sha:` manifest step (signing.py L228-231) was shaped as an 'in-toto-style subject so a future move to a DSSE/asymmetric envelope is an envelope swap.' The plan neither acknowledges in-toto link `materials` fields nor SLSA provenance `byProducts`/`materials` as considered alternatives, nor justifies why pins are bespoke plaintext lines rather than structured materials inside the existing DSSE predicate.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear … plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>'
scenarios: A future migration to an asymmetric DSSE envelope (already anticipated for verified-at-sha) requires bespoke parser surgery for pin lines, while an in-toto materials field would already be in the right shape.
An external SLSA or in-toto verifier cannot interpret plan-material pins without a bespoke parser, whereas structured materials would be understood natively.
The plan adds a ManifestFormatError parser for pin lines that is entirely absent from the existing substrate, duplicating structure the DSSE/in-toto predicate already provides.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add a sentence such as: 'In-toto link materials fields and SLSA provenance byProducts were considered but rejected because: (a) the existing manifest channel already serves additive signing without a new envelope kind; (b) the HMAC substrate cannot carry a structured predicate today — the verified-at-sha precedent is the established pattern; (c) the ManifestFormatError parser provides equivalent tamper-detection within the signed bytes.' If the rationale cannot be articulated, consider encoding pins as a structured field in the DSSE predicate instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rationale in the Implementation section for why `tracker_head_sha()` cannot delegate to `run_git()` (already importable at the `llm/` layer) with an incremental dirty/unmerged check, rather than reimplementing git subprocess invocations from scratch.
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py L41-63: snapshot_head(repo_root) runs git rev-parse against the tickets branch/HEAD and returns the 40-hex SHA, with EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL fallback — 20+ callers

src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py L26-70: run_git() is the project-wide git subprocess wrapper, introduced to eliminate 'a dozen hand-rolled wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract'

src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py L25: 'from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git' — the llm/ layer already imports run_git, so the plan's restriction ('imports no signing or orchestration modules') does not block using it
Plan quote: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse' — names snapshot_head as the prior art it is NOT reusing, but gives no rationale
ABSENCE: the plan does not explain why the dirty/unmerged check could not be layered atop snapshot_head() or delegated through run_git()
findings.finding: The plan specifies `tracker_head_sha()` as 'new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse' — but the existing `snapshot_head()` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py` already performs git rev-parse against the tracker and returns a 40-hex SHA (with tested fallback/empty-repo handling), and the existing `run_git()` in `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py` is the project-wide shared git subprocess wrapper already importable at the `llm/` layer (`src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py` imports it). The plan does not engage with either prior art when specifying tracker_head_sha(), creating a risk of duplicate git-subprocess logic with divergent edge-case handling.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u'
scenarios: Implementer writes a fresh subprocess block for git status/ls-files/rev-parse, recreating the fragmentation run_git was designed to eliminate.
Edge-case handling diverges from snapshot_head's tested EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL contract, causing test divergence on bare repos.
A future change to run_git (timeout, tracing, index-lock retry) does not propagate to tracker_head_sha(), creating a silent behavioral split.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that tracker_head_sha() calls run_git(tracker, 'status', '--porcelain'), run_git(tracker, 'ls-files', '-u'), and run_git(tracker, 'rev-parse', 'HEAD') rather than rolling new subprocess calls, so the existing error-handling contract (timeout, text mode, CalledProcessError mapping) is inherited automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit field specification for `PlanMaterialPin` in the relation_snapshot.py definition: enumerate its fields (`role: Literal['child', 'prerequisite']`, `canonical_id: str`, `material_fingerprint: str`) and its container type (dataclass or NamedTuple).
evidence: Plan: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py'
Plan: 'related_material is a deterministic tuple of {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} records' — field names appear only here, not in the PlanMaterialPin definition.
No field types, no container type (dataclass vs NamedTuple), and no role Literal constraint are specified for PlanMaterialPin.
findings.finding: The `PlanMaterialPin` dataclass fields are never explicitly enumerated in the plan. The plan names the type in 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py' and states that `related_material` is a deterministic tuple of `{role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint}` records — but whether `PlanMaterialPin` is exactly those three fields (and what their Python types are) is only inferrable from the manifest line format and the `related_material` description. A reader of just the relation_snapshot.py definition paragraph cannot determine whether `PlanMaterialPin` is a dataclass, NamedTuple, TypedDict, or plain dict; or whether `role` is a Literal type constrained to the two-value vocabulary.
findings.location: Implementation §2 (relation_snapshot.py definition)
scenarios: An implementor using a plain dict for PlanMaterialPin produces a different public surface than attest.py re-exports — tests expecting attribute access (pin.role) will fail.

The role field left as `str` rather than `Literal['child', 'prerequisite']` means manifest_pins() must redundantly validate it, but the plan's ManifestFormatError list already covers 'unknown role' at parse time — creating unclear responsibility split.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation §2: 'PlanMaterialPin is a frozen dataclass (or NamedTuple) with fields role: Literal["child", "prerequisite"], canonical_id: str, material_fingerprint: str (exactly 16 lowercase hex characters).'

findings.finding: The terms 'enforcement enabled' and 'enforcement disabled' appear in ACs 17-18 and the implementation ('Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled') but the plan never defines what 'enforcement' is — what flag or setting controls it, which module owns it, or how a test toggles it. An executor cannot write the AC 17-18 tests without knowing the flag's identity, type, and location.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines 17-18 ('Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership', 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership') and Implementation bullet 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Enforcement refers to the boolean flag plan_review.config.PREREQUISITE_ENFORCEMENT_ENABLED (or equivalent); tests toggle it via monkeypatch or a constructor argument to the signing path under test.'

findings.finding: The 'enforcement enabled/disabled' decision rule is referenced in ACs 17-18 and the Implementation ('Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled') but is never defined — no config key, flag name, code path, or enum vocabulary specifying what 'enforcement' means or how it is toggled.
findings.location: Implementation §5 ('Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled'); AC lines 17-18 ('Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership', 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence naming the control: e.g. 'Enforcement is the `require_plan_material_pins` config boolean (default False on this branch; True after child 5967 activates it). With enforcement disabled, pin collection still runs and pins are still emitted, but a PlanRelationSnapshotError does not block signing — it is logged and the review proceeds pinless.'

findings.finding: The Implementation section simultaneously asserts two mutually contradictory activation semantics: (a) 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot…immediately before building its new manifest' and 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless', yet (b) 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths.' Statement (a) implies pin collection is wired and mandatory in this story; statement (b) implies it is deferred and optional. The ACs then affirm (a): 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set' (AC 19). Implementers will receive conflicting directives.
findings.location: Implementation section (paragraph 5 and 6) vs AC lines 19-21
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths' with 'pin collection is wired into all signing paths in this story; the feature branch is not merged to main until child 5967 completes the atomic integration tests, but the code paths are live on the feature branch and covered by this story's tests.'

findings.finding: The plan contains an unresolved ambiguity about which signing paths actually emit pins after this story lands versus after child 5967. It simultaneously states (a) 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot … immediately before building its new manifest and passes related_material' (this story's Implementation) and (b) 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths.' If this story wires the collection calls into all signing paths, then pins ARE being emitted after this story — making child 5967's 'atomic activation' role unclear. If child 5967 is the actual activation switch, then this story does NOT wire the calls into production signing paths — contradicting (a). The plan never clarifies the mechanism by which pin collection is 'optional' when the collection call is unconditional.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot … immediately before building its new manifest' vs. 'this story lands only on the epic feature branch: optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths'

findings.finding: The plan contains an internal contradiction on whether pins are enforced in this story: one sentence states 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless,' but a later sentence states 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths.' An implementer reading both will not know whether this story makes pins mandatory or optional, and the ACs ('Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set') do not resolve whether that emission is enforced at signing time or only present as optional infrastructure.
findings.location: Implementation section — paragraphs 5 and 6; AC lines 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set', 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single explicit sentence such as: 'On this feature branch, all three signing paths (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, resign_plan_review) collect and emit pins unconditionally; the phrase "optional pins preserve old behavior" refers only to the main-branch state before child 5967 merges this branch — not to any code path in this story.' Then update the AC to read 'Ordinary signing on the feature branch emits the complete pin set' to make the scope unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what guarantee (if any) `ticket_store_revision` provides in this story's intermediate implementation — before child 5967's atomic lock-verify-sign — and document whether verifiers should treat it as advisory or binding while the feature branch is incomplete.
evidence: 'This story verifies accurate token capture only; it never acquires the sign lock.'
'Child 5967 exclusively owns before/after mismatch retries, under-lock HEAD re-verification, atomic append+commit, structured retry logging, and exhaustion behavior.'
'Child 5967 does not activate or duplicate collection; it replaces the direct pre-sign step with the unified atomic snapshot/retry transaction around the already-wired calls.'

The plan wires collection before signing but the lock that would make capture+sign atomic is not acquired in this story, leaving ticket_store_revision unconstrained relative to the actual store at sign time.
findings.finding: The plan wires `tracker_head_sha()` snapshot collection (including `ticket_store_revision`) into every signing path in this story, but explicitly defers the under-lock HEAD re-verification and atomic append+commit to child 5967. This creates a TOCTOU window — the tracker HEAD is read, then the sign lock is acquired separately (or not at all in this story), and `ticket_store_revision` in the signed manifest may reflect a store state that has already advanced. The plan does not specify what guarantee verifiers or downstream consumers should attach to `ticket_store_revision` while child 5967 is absent, nor what behavior is correct when a consumer reads a signed manifest whose `ticket_store_revision` is stale.
findings.location: Implementation §5 — tracker_head_sha and §6 — 'Child 5967 exclusively owns before/after mismatch retries, under-lock HEAD re-verification, atomic append+commit'
scenarios: A verifier that treats ticket_store_revision as a freshness bound reads a signed manifest produced by this story's implementation and observes a SHA that predates a store commit that added a new blocker — the verifier incorrectly concludes the snapshot was fresh when it was not.

A signing operation runs on a CI server where the tracker is updated concurrently; the captured HEAD SHA is 3 commits behind the actual HEAD at the moment the manifest is written, producing a signed manifest with a stale ticket_store_revision that no current code will detect or retry until child 5967 lands.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Implementation section that `ticket_store_revision` captured in this story is best-effort (not lock-verified), and mark it as advisory in the manifest format spec until child 5967 upgrades it to a lock-verified value. Alternatively, have this story omit `ticket_store_revision` from the signed manifest until child 5967 can guarantee its correctness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured log entry (e.g., event: plan_relation_snapshot_collected, ticket_count, duration_ms, revision) emitted on successful pin collection so operators can monitor O(store) preload performance and detect regressions.
evidence: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states; this explicit O(store) map is required' — O(store) operation on every signing call
Structured ERROR record is specified only for the failure path (plan_relation_snapshot_error event); no analogous log/metric for successful collection or collection duration
'tracker_head_sha ... run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u' — multiple subprocess calls per signing operation with no timing instrumentation
findings.finding: The plan adds a potentially expensive O(store) ticket-directory reduction on every signing path but specifies no instrumentation (log, metric, or trace) for the happy-path performance of pin collection, making it impossible to detect regressions or latency spikes in production without a traceback-level failure.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review'; Verification section
scenarios: Ticket store grows to hundreds of tickets; signing latency increases silently with no observable signal until user complaints arise.
A slow filesystem or network-mounted tracker causes collection to take seconds per signing call; operators have no metric to detect or alert on this.
A regression in preload efficiency introduced by a future story goes unnoticed until end-to-end latency is obviously degraded.
findings.suggested_fix: In collect_plan_relation_snapshot, after the store-wide reduction completes, emit a structured log record with at minimum: event name, number of tickets scanned, wall-clock duration, and ticket_store_revision. This requires no new dependency and aligns with the existing structured-log pattern used for the error path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether tracker_head_sha() can be implemented by composing existing `run_git` (already used in the llm layer via code_review/assemble.py) with the new `status --porcelain` / `ls-files -u` / `rev-parse HEAD` calls, rather than re-implementing git subprocess handling from scratch.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py line 25: 'from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git' — run_git is already imported in the llm layer
src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py: run_git() is the one shared git subprocess wrapper covering rev-parse, status, ls-files, error handling, check=, timeout= parameters
src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py lines 41-63: snapshot_head() already performs git rev-parse on the tickets branch HEAD with error handling
Plan text: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u'
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() in relation_snapshot.py rebuilds git subprocess invocation logic (status --porcelain, ls-files -u, rev-parse HEAD, error-catching) that run_git() in rebar._store.gitutil already provides, and which is already importable at the llm/ layer (src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py imports run_git directly). The plan explicitly marks it 'new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse' but does not justify why composing run_git with the new cleanliness checks is insufficient.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse'
scenarios: The new tracker_head_sha() will duplicate run_git's subprocess.run wrapping, timeout handling, and CalledProcessError catching — any bug fixes to run_git must be applied separately to tracker_head_sha().
If run_git gains a new env-variable injection or security hardening, tracker_head_sha() will not inherit it.
findings.suggested_fix: In relation_snapshot.py, import run_git from rebar._store.gitutil (as code_review/assemble.py already does) and call run_git(tracker, 'status', '--porcelain'), run_git(tracker, 'ls-files', '-u'), and run_git(tracker, 'rev-parse', 'HEAD') directly, wrapping each in the required PlanRelationSnapshotError translation. This eliminates the NIH duplication while preserving all the new error-handling semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether an `include_archived` parameter on find_inbound_relationships (flipping line 103 of _inbound.py) achieves the archived-inbound-source discovery AC, allowing relation_snapshot.py to avoid a full O(store) preload and instead use the existing two-phase byte-scan approach.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py line 103: 'if state.get("status") in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get("archived"): continue' — the sole reason find_inbound_relationships misses archived tickets
src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py lines 39-72: _mentioning_dirs() performs O(byte-scan) to find only mentioning directories before reducing, avoiding full-store reduce
Plan: 'this explicit O(store) map is required to discover arbitrary inbound blockers' — but _inbound.py's byte-scan approach already limits reduction to mentioning dirs
AC: 'Archived inbound-block sources remain discoverable' — the only behavioral delta from _inbound.py's existing filter
findings.finding: The plan mandates a full O(store) reduce_all_tickets-equivalent preload — 'reducing every ticket directory, including archived states' — to build ticket_states_by_id, explicitly rejecting find_inbound_relationships because 'its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets.' However, the only thing find_inbound_relationships does that the plan wants to avoid is line 103 of _inbound.py: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue`. Adding an `include_archived` flag to _inbound.py's reduce loop would achieve archived-source discovery while preserving the O(byte-scan)+O(reduce-mentioning) efficiency.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 2: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states… do not call find_inbound_relationships because its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets.'
scenarios: For a store with 1000 tickets where 2 mention the subject, the plan's approach reduces all 1000 tickets; _inbound.py with include_archived=True would byte-scan 1000 dirs and reduce only the 2 mentioning ones.
Plan also needs ticket_states_by_id for target readability checks, but those targets are bounded by the subject's own dep list — not the full store.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether PlanRelationSnapshot needs to be a named dataclass with a typed boundary now (for child 7c27's future use), or whether an inline named-tuple / dict at the signing seam is sufficient until 7c27 is ready to define its own input contract.
evidence: Plan text: 'this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists' — explicitly future consumer
Plan defines PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() as distinct types in a new relation_snapshot.py module for this one story's use

The three signing paths (sign, refresh, resign) all follow the identical pattern: call collect_plan_relation_snapshot → pass related_material → build_manifest — one functional call-site pattern, not three independent consumers
Rule-of-Three: abstraction warranted at >=3 independent call-sites; at delivery there is 1 functional pattern + 1 future hypothetical consumer
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshot is introduced as a typed boundary 'for the future prerequisite criterion' (child 7c27), but child 7c27 'does not implement or assume that criterion exists.' At delivery time of this story, the signing paths (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, resign_plan_review) form one functional call-site cluster — they all call collect_plan_relation_snapshot and immediately pass related_material to build_manifest. The only named second consumer is future child 7c27. Defining a standalone typed dataclass for a consumer that does not yet exist is a Rule-of-Three violation.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 2: 'Child 7c27 owns registration, schemas, prompts, and routing for the future prerequisite criterion; this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists.'
scenarios: If child 7c27 is never delivered or is redesigned, PlanRelationSnapshot becomes dead abstraction weight.
The typing boundary adds indirection (collect → snapshot → build_manifest) that could be expressed as a direct function call if child 7c27 didn't exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `tracker_head_sha` should restrict its dirty-check to modified/staged/unmerged content only (e.g. by filtering `git status --porcelain` to exclude `??`-prefixed untracked-file lines, or using `git diff --quiet HEAD` instead), distinguishing 'working tree has uncommitted changes to tracked files' from 'working tree has untracked files'.
evidence: `git status --porcelain` outputs `?? <file>` for untracked files in the working tree, not only for modified/staged files.
A tracker directory containing any untracked file (e.g. `.DS_Store`, editor swap files, or CI build artifacts) would trigger store-read-failure even though the committed tickets state is clean.
The acceptance criterion 'Dirty, unmerged, subprocess, IO, path, or malformed-output failures map to store-read-failure' does not distinguish untracked files from dirty/unmerged files.
findings.finding: `tracker_head_sha`'s 'any output raises store-read-failure' rule applied to `git status --porcelain` will fire on untracked files (reported as `??` lines), causing false store-read-failure for legitimate tracker directories that happen to contain untracked files (e.g. `.DS_Store`, build artifacts, or any file not committed to the tickets branch). This is a hidden failure case in the mechanism.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u; any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure)'
scenarios: macOS tracker directories containing `.DS_Store` untracked files would abort every review with INDETERMINATE.
CI environments where the tracker directory has temporary build artifacts or test output files would cause spurious store-read-failure.
A developer with any new untracked file in the tracker repo would be unable to produce a signed review until the file is removed or .gitignored.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `git status --porcelain` with `git status --porcelain -- '*.json'` or filter out lines starting with `??`, or use `git diff --quiet HEAD` to detect only modifications to tracked files. Keep `git ls-files -u` for unmerged detection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the tracker_head_sha() porcelain check to exclude untracked-file lines ('??' prefix), or replace git status --porcelain with a command that reports only dirty/staged entries (e.g., git status --porcelain | grep -v '^??'), to prevent false store-read-failure on trackers with legitimate untracked files.
evidence: Plan text: 'run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u; any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure, reference=<tracker>)'

tests/_isolation.py lines 54-57: porcelain() uses git status --porcelain and its return value is described as 'the set of git status --porcelain lines … Compare two snapshots to find what a run added to the working tree' — untracked '??' lines included
tests/conftest.py lines 388-414: the test harness explicitly uses git status --porcelain to find 'new dirty entry' and notes gitignored paths are excluded but untracked non-ignored paths are included
Partially-verified: git status --porcelain emits '?? path' for untracked, non-ignored files — this is standard git behavior corroborated by the codebase's own use of the command
findings.finding: The plan specifies that in tracker_head_sha(), 'run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain … any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure)', but git status --porcelain emits '?? <path>' lines for untracked files — not only for dirty (modified/staged) or unmerged entries. A tracker worktree that happens to contain any untracked file (temp files, editor swap files, test artifacts) would trigger a false store-read-failure, blocking every plan-review signing operation until the untracked file is removed. The codebase explicitly documents this: _isolation.py line 54-56 uses git status --porcelain to detect 'what a run added to the working tree', confirming the team knows untracked files appear in porcelain output.
findings.location: Implementation section, tracker_head_sha() specification: 'run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain … any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError'; AC: 'Dirty, unmerged, subprocess, IO, path, or malformed-output failures map to store-read-failure'
scenarios: A developer runs rebar sign/refresh on a ticket in a repo where the tickets tracker has any untracked scratch file — signing fails with store-read-failure instead of succeeding.
CI environments that write untracked temp files into the tracker directory between operations would cause all signing to fail.
Integration tests that leave untracked artifacts in the tracker worktree would turn every signing call into a false store-read-failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Filter the porcelain output: treat only lines NOT starting with '??' as indicating a dirty worktree (i.e., raise store-read-failure only when `[line for line in output.splitlines() if line and not line.startswith('??')]` is non-empty). Alternatively, use `git diff --quiet HEAD` (exits non-zero only for modified tracked content) to separate dirty-tracked from untracked detection.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
